label: Slothrop / Rocket Pursuit / Scattering
arc_description:
Slothrop's plotline is the novel's longest continuous thread and its most structurally paradoxical: the line that governs the most episodes is also the line that narrates its own dissolution. In Part 1, the rocket-body correlation establishes itself through institutional attention — Slothrop's sexual map coincides with V-2 impact sites, making him simultaneously the object of Pointsman's Pavlovian programme and the protagonist of a quest he has not yet chosen. The thread function is 'establishes' and 'recurs,' the body shuttling between correlation and conditioning without yet becoming a pursuer.

Part 2 converts Slothrop from object to agent: every episode runs the 'pursues' function at high or maximum intensity as he chases rocket intelligence across the Riviera Casino. But the pursuit is already compromised — Pointsman's apparatus and Katje's double role mean that each act of investigation is simultaneously an act of being handled. The corporate-military web saturates these episodes; Slothrop pursues through power structures, not around them.

Part 3 is the arc's long transformation and collapse. The pursuit continues through the Zone's first episodes (3.01–3.04) but the thread function shifts to 'transforms' at 3.06 when the Rocketman costume appears at Neubabelsberg — identity becoming staged performance rather than driven quest. By 3.21, 'scatters' replaces 'pursues' as the governing function, and the protagonist begins dissolving: into aliases, into the Zone's geography, into Puritan genealogy (3.25), into pop-song fragments (3.31). The scattering is not a sudden event but a long structural diminuendo across twelve episodes, during which Slothrop's intensity oscillates between background and maximum without ever stabilising.

Part 4 delivers the final arc. Slothrop appears only at background intensity in most episodes, his scattering now a settled condition rather than an ongoing drama. The single exception is 4.06, where the scattering surges to maximum alongside the novel's highest intensity of elect-preterite theology — a terminal statement that reframes dissolution not as narrative failure but as the Puritan theology's logical conclusion: the preterite body cannot be saved, only scattered. In 4.12, Slothrop's line appears at moderate intensity in the terminal episode, one thread among four converging in the Orpheus Theatre's frozen descent.

label: Blicero / Katje / Gottfried / 00000
arc_description:
The Blicero plotline operates on a different temporal logic than any other line in the novel: it appears in concentrated bursts separated by long structural absences, giving it the quality of a recurring vision or fairy-tale interpolation rather than continuous narrative. Its total episode count is modest, but its intensity at each appearance is consistently high, and its terminal episodes carry the novel's greatest structural weight.

The line establishes itself in a single hinge episode — 1.14 — at maximum intensity, introducing the erotic-sacrificial command structure at the V-2 battery in occupied Europe. Blicero, Katje, and Gottfried appear fully formed: the fairy-tale script (Hansel-and-Gretel inverted), the erotic domination binding commander to victims, and the 00000 rocket as terminal parabola are all present from the first appearance. The species-extinction register sounds alongside them, scaling personal sacrifice to planetary dimension.

Through Part 2, the line recurs at low-to-moderate intensity as Katje's presence at the Casino carries Blicero's residue into the Pointsman-Slothrop apparatus — she is the structural bridge between P02 and P05, her double role as handler and survivor keeping both plotlines in contact. In Part 3, the line submerges for long stretches before converging at maximum intensity in 3.17, where Blicero's command narrative tightens aboard the Anubis alongside the film-body transformation, fusing erotic command with mediated performance.

Part 4 is the sacrificial terminus. Episode 4.05 — a hinge — delivers the 00000 launch with Gottfried sealed inside the Imipolex shroud, three modes peaking simultaneously at maximum. The line sustains high intensity through 4.09–4.10 as the sacrifice reverberates across the Schwarzkommando's parallel 00001 assembly, and reaches its terminal statement in 4.12, where the descent into the Orpheus Theatre holds the rocket's parabola frozen above the audience. The plotline's arc is not development but revelation: Blicero's command was always already terminal, and each recurrence strips away one more layer of the fairy-tale apparatus to expose the sacrificial logic underneath.

label: Enzian / Schwarzkommando / 00001
arc_description:
The Schwarzkommando plotline is the novel's primary colonial-historical line and its most sustained ethical argument. It is entirely absent from Parts 1 and 2 — Enzian and the Herero survivors do not appear until the Zone opens in Part 3 — giving the plotline the structural force of a delayed revelation: the colonial history that subtends the rocket programme was present all along but invisible within the London and Riviera institutional frames.

The line establishes itself at maximum intensity in 3.03, with the colonial-genocide register immediately at its highest level. This is not a gradual emergence but a full structural irruption: Herero survival, Schwarzkommando technical expertise, the Ombindi faction's internal debate about whether assembling the 00001 rocket constitutes continuation or self-erasure — all are present from the first episode. The establishment continues through 3.04 alongside Slothrop and Tchitcherine, braiding the colonial line into the Zone's multiple pursuit frameworks.

Through middle Part 3, the line alternates between survival and background recurrence (3.05, 3.21, 3.26, 3.27), persisting at moderate intensity while other plotlines — Slothrop's transformation, the Anubis film-body sequence — take structural priority. The hinge comes at 3.22, where the revelation function brings the full historical weight of colonial genocide and species extinction to structural legibility, with the richest modal density since the novel's opening.

Part 4 delivers convergence. The function shifts from 'survives' to 'converges' across 4.04, 4.07, 4.10, and 4.12, the 00001 rocket assembly approaching completion. In 4.10, the Schwarzkommando convergence reaches maximum alongside colonial-genocide recurrence at peak and terminal-rocket-descent at maximum — the structural mirror of Blicero's 4.05 sacrifice, 00001 answering 00000. The novel's terminal episode (4.12) holds both lines at maximum intensity, the two parabolas — colonial extermination and colonial survival — aimed at the same sky without resolution.

label: Pökler / Leni / Ilse / Jamf / Imipolex
arc_description:
The Pökler plotline carries the novel's industrial-historical argument: the inseparability of family life, chemical manufacture, and forced-labour infrastructure. It appears in concentrated bursts rather than continuous narrative, each burst deepening the entanglement between personal memory and systemic complicity. Uniquely among the eight plotlines, it completes its arc entirely within Parts 1–3 and does not appear in Part 4 — its terminal statement is Dora, not the final rocket descent.

Part 1 delivers two establishing episodes at maximum intensity (1.11, 1.19) separated by a moderate recurrence (1.13). The first introduces Jamf's chemistry and the Imipolex connection; the second activates forced-labour and Holocaust-adjacency registers for the first time, with waste/body-grotesque at maximum. A third recurrence at moderate intensity closes Part 1 (1.21). The pattern is already set: Pökler's line arrives in spikes of high intensity that connect personal-familial material (Leni, Ilse) to industrial-chemical infrastructure (Jamf, Imipolex, rocket manufacture).

Part 2 maintains the line at moderate-to-high intensity through two episodes (2.05, 2.07), the latter shifting the thread function to 'reveals' as the full scope of the Jamf-Imipolex connection becomes legible alongside Slothrop's pursuit. The corporate-military web peaks, binding Pökler's industrial memory to the Zone's wider power structures.

Part 3 is the plotline's structural climax and terminus. The hinge episode 3.11 — one of the novel's two most structurally dense episodes — delivers Pökler to Dora/Mittelwerk, where the mourning function operates at maximum alongside forced-labour and technocratic-continuation registers at their peak. This is the moment where individual engineering memory confronts the camp infrastructure it served. The revelation continues in 3.13, where four historical-recurrence registers activate simultaneously — the highest density in the novel. The second hinge (3.29) delivers the line's final maximum-intensity mourning, completing the arc: family loss has become inseparable from the material logic of rocket production, and the complicity is irrevocable.

label: Pointsman / White Visitation / Control
arc_description:
The Pointsman plotline is the novel's institutional-scientific line and its most structurally compressed arc: it dominates Part 1, pursues through Part 2, and dies at Part 2's close, never appearing again. This compression gives the line a parabolic shape that mirrors the rocket — rise, peak, and terminal descent — enacted through bureaucratic rather than ballistic physics.

Part 1 is Pointsman's territory. The line establishes itself across nearly every episode from 1.03 to 1.20, sustaining moderate-to-maximum intensity through the functions 'establishes' and 'reveals.' The White Visitation's Pavlovian apparatus is the structural engine of Part 1: Slothrop's body is converted into experimental datum, Roger Mexico's statistics provide the interpretive framework, and the science-control mode peaks repeatedly alongside sexuality-domination, revealing behavioural science and erotic power as twin instruments of the same institutional programme. The line's maximum-intensity episodes (1.06, 1.07, 1.08) are the novel's purest expressions of the control thesis: the body under observation is the body under command.

Part 2 converts establishment to pursuit. Pointsman's programme follows Slothrop to the Riviera through agents and controlled information, sustaining moderate intensity in most episodes before surging to maximum in 2.04, where the technocratic-continuation register sounds for the first time — the apparatus is already reorganising for peacetime.

The terminal episode is 2.08, where the line achieves maximum intensity through the function 'fails.' The White Visitation's organisational death is the novel's first plotline closure: Pavlovian logic cannot survive the transition from wartime institution to Zone chaos. The failure is structural rather than personal — Pointsman does not lose a contest but presides over the collapse of the institutional conditions that made his programme possible. After 2.08, the plotline vanishes entirely, its absence from Parts 3 and 4 as structurally meaningful as its dominance of Part 1.

label: Pirate / Roger / Jessica / Counterforce
arc_description:
The Counterforce plotline traces the novel's most sustained arc of hope and its most thoroughgoing demonstration of hope's failure. It is the only line that appears in all four parts, and its thread functions narrate a complete trajectory: establishes, survives, survives, fails, mourns, parodies. The arc is not a decline from strength but a long survival that eventually proves insufficient.

Part 1 establishes the line through its intimate register: Roger and Jessica's wartime love, Pirate Prentice's comic-domestic presence, the statistical scepticism and pop-song warmth that constitute the preterite's counter-knowledge. Three establishing episodes (1.02, 1.05, 1.09) run at maximum intensity, with survival episodes (1.06, 1.16) sustaining the line between them. The Counterforce is not yet an organisation — it is a quality of attention, a way of being beside the rockets rather than inside their logic.

Part 2 is notable for the line's complete absence: it does not appear in any of the eight Riviera episodes. The institutional-pursuit framework of Part 2 has no space for the Counterforce's intimate register; Roger and Jessica belong to London's wartime texture, not the Casino's operational games.

Part 3 brings the line back through two consecutive maximum-intensity survival episodes (3.23, 3.24), now explicitly organisational — the Counterforce as named entity rather than ambient quality. The comedy-song mode provides its primary register, but the doubled presence also marks the line's peak visibility before collapse.

Part 4 converts survival to failure across a compressed sequence. The line fails at maximum intensity in 4.02 as the corporate-military web proves structurally dominant, then fades to background in 4.03. The mourning function arrives in 4.08 at maximum intensity — Roger Mexico's grief marking the personal cost of organisational impossibility. The arc's final function is parody (4.09), resistance surviving only as comic inversion of what it once attempted, its remnant absorbed into the very structures it opposed.

label: Tchitcherine / Geli / Soviet Counterline
arc_description:
Tchitcherine's plotline is the novel's intelligence-bureaucratic line and its most compressed pursuit arc. Entirely absent from Parts 1 and 2, it appears only in the Zone episodes of Parts 3 and 4, running parallel to the Schwarzkommando line without ever achieving the recognition that would resolve it. Its structural function is to demonstrate that kinship — Tchitcherine and Enzian are half-brothers — can be negated by institutional classification.

The line enters at moderate intensity in 3.04 alongside Slothrop and Enzian, immediately establishing the Zone as a space of competing state-intelligence frameworks. It reaches maximum pursuit intensity in 3.05 through the Central Asian analepsis, deepening the Soviet bureaucratic apparatus as a rival system of rocket-knowledge, and again in 3.09. Through middle and late Part 3 (3.10, 3.27, 3.32), the line alternates between background and moderate intensity, the pursuit continuing without resolution.

Part 4 delivers the line's transformation and dissolution. Episode 4.07 — maximum intensity, function 'transforms' — is the structural climax: Tchitcherine encounters Enzian but does not recognise him, Geli Tripping's organic counter-magic having dissolved the bureaucratic categories that defined the pursuit. The unrecognition is the plotline's terminal event: not confrontation or reconciliation but the quiet cancellation of a pursuit that depended on institutional knowledge to identify its object. The line persists at reduced intensity through 4.09 and 4.11 but its structural work is complete — the Soviet counterline has demonstrated that state-intelligence pursuit, like Pointsman's Pavlovian programme, cannot survive contact with the Zone's dissolution of institutional frames.

label: Film / Body / Image / Greta / Bianca / von Göll
arc_description:
The film-body plotline operates unlike any other line in the braid: rather than tracing a character's journey or an institution's programme, it tracks a mode of relation — the conversion of bodies into images, performances, and vulnerable surfaces. Its structural behaviour is archipelagic, appearing in clusters of high-intensity episodes separated by stretches of absence, and its character anchors (Greta Erdmann, Bianca, Gerhardt von Göll, Thanatz) function less as continuous protagonists than as recurring vessels for the image-logic the plotline carries.

The line is entirely absent from Parts 1 and 2. Its structural territory is Part 3, where it appears in sixteen of thirty-two episodes, creating the novel's densest plotline cluster outside of P01. The arc moves through three distinct phases: environmental presence at Neubabelsberg (3.06–3.07), where film-studio setting gives the mode its first structural foothold; pure film-logic in 3.08, the line's only solo-command episode; and the Anubis sequence (3.14–3.18), where the plotline reaches maximum intensity across five consecutive episodes, shifting from recurrence through transformation to sustained recurrence. The Anubis is the line's structural centre — a floating stage where bodies become surfaces, testimony is indistinguishable from performance, and Greta's history converts physical vulnerability into narrative material.

The transformation function (3.15–3.17) marks the plotline's deepest intervention: alongside Blicero's convergence in 3.17, the film-body line fuses mediated identity with erotic-sacrificial command, producing the novel's most explicit demonstration that image-logic and rocket-logic operate through the same structures of domination.

Part 4 reduces the line to afterimage and terminal recurrence. Episode 4.01 registers the Bianca aftermath at background intensity, and the line disappears until 4.12, where it recurs at moderate intensity in the Orpheus Theatre — a movie house, the novel's final image-space, where the rocket's descent is suspended above an audience that has become both spectators and targets.
